summary:
Solomon's Temple (First Temple, traditionally c. 957 BC – 586 BC) was the central sanctuary of ancient Israelite religion in Jerusalem — according to the biblical account, built by King Solomon using materials supplied by Hiram of Tyre, featuring a cedar-lined interior, the Holy of Holies containing the Ark of the Covenant, and elaborately decorated bronze furnishings. It served as the earthly dwelling place of the God of Israel and the focal point of the sacrificial cult.

The Temple's precise location, size, and features are known primarily from biblical descriptions in 1 Kings 6–8 and parallel texts in Chronicles, corroborated partly by archaeological evidence of 10th-century BC monumental construction in Jerusalem. The Temple Mount (Haram al-Sharif) where it stood remains one of the most contested religious sites in the world — claimed by Judaism, Islam (the Dome of the Rock and Al-Aqsa Mosque were built there), and Christianity. Its [[event:1909|Destruction of Solomon Temple]] by Nebuchadnezzar in 586 BC was a defining trauma for Israelite religion; the Second Temple rebuilt in 516 BC was itself destroyed by Rome in 70 AD. The Temple remains the central symbol of Jewish religious longing and identity, with the Western Wall (part of the Temple Mount retaining wall built by Herod) as the holiest accessible site in Judaism.
